summary:
The Dutch Revolt (also the Eighty Years' War, 1568–1648 AD) was the successful rebellion of the seventeen Dutch provinces against Spanish Habsburg rule — one of the first successful national liberation movements in modern history, producing the independent Dutch Republic (the United Provinces) and creating the conditions for the [[event:2326|Dutch Golden Age]]. The revolt was triggered by the combination of Spanish fiscal demands (the hated 'Tenth Penny' tax on commerce), Philip II's aggressive Counter-Reformation enforcement (including the Inquisition), and the military repression under the Duke of Alba (who executed approximately 18,000 people as 'heretics' and rebels between 1567–1573). William of Orange ('William the Silent'), a Catholic who converted to Calvinism, became the revolt's political and military leader.

The revolt's early decades were nearly fatal: Spanish armies repeatedly reconquered rebel territory, and William of Orange was assassinated in 1584 by a Catholic fanatic. The decisive turn came when the southern provinces (roughly modern Belgium) were reconquered by Spain and remained Catholic, while the northern provinces — Holland, Zeeland, Utrecht, and others — signed the Union of Utrecht (1579) and declared independence as the Republic of the Seven United Netherlands (1581). The [[event:2434|Eighty Years' War]] continued until the [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]] (1648), which formally recognized Dutch independence. The Dutch Republic's unique political structure — a federation of merchant oligarchies governed through the States-General, without a hereditary monarch — combined with its commercial culture and Calvinist work ethic to produce the extraordinary economic and cultural flowering of the Golden Age. The revolt's success demonstrated that organized resistance to imperial Catholic power was possible, inspiring Protestants across Europe.
